text: Thank you very much. I'm Vahag Nicolian from the Oregon Health and Science University, and I'm presenting on behalf of my team.
This is a case of a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair in a patient with an infrapubic penile prosthesis.
Disclosures, Dr. Orenstein and myself serve as instructors and consultants for a variety of groups, though these are not relevant to today's conversation.
This was an eighty-eight-year-old man who presented with a history of atrial fibrillation and infrapubic penile prosthesis and a symptomatic recurrent right inguinal hernia.
His hernia history was notable for an emergent right inguinal hernia repair with mesh for incarceration. The operative report described a blown-out floor and a high likelihood to recur.
He had had an immediate recurrence within weeks of the operation and had multiple visits to the ED for pain and partial incarcerations.

When operating on patients with infrapubic penile implants, it's important to understand the components and the type of implant in place. This patient had a scrotal pump device with a reservoir placed in the space of retzius.

In these particular cases, the tubing connecting the reservoir to the system can exit suprapubic or through the external ring. The term infrapubic penile implant is actually referring to the incision that was made in the skin when the implant was placed, not necessarily related to the tubing.
It's a relative contraindication to MIS inguinal hernia repair, and we'll show why.
The CT scan image reveals a retro rectus reservoir, indicating the location of the reservoir in relation to the rectus muscles.
You see inguinal scrotal hernia, and you saw some anterior mesh. Inguinal hernia repair for patients with infrapubic penile implants is usually done in the open approach. It's just safer.
However, this patient had had a recurrence, so we elected to move forward with MIS.

When performing an MIS repair, it's important to be prepared for scarring that can occur with the reservoir to the bladder, the epigastrics, as well as the external iliac vessels, and have contingencies in place so that you can effectively manage these should they be injured. Mesh placement can be impacted because of the reservoir, so be aware that you may need to modify the mesh or remove the prosthesis at the time of the operation.
The surgeon begins the procedure by creating a peritoneal flap, exposing the underlying anatomical structures for further surgical intervention.
The surgeon develops the peritoneal flap and proceeds with a lateral first dissection due to the presence of a retro rectus reservoir, deviating from the usual medial first approach.
The surgeon begins the dissection in the pre-transversalis plane, later transitioning to re-establish a pre-peritoneal dissection, revealing the anatomical layers involved in the procedure.
The surgeon works towards the indirect hernia sac, demonstrating the reduction of a moderate-sized inguinal scrotal hernia.
The surgeon identifies the epigastric vessels medially and develops a larger lateral pocket to accommodate an extra-large mesh during the hernia repair.
The surgeon begins the medial dissection, encountering scarring around the reservoir that appears to be adhered to the transversalis fascia.
The surgeon carefully dissects and removes the initial scarring layer surrounding the encapsulated plastic reservoir, revealing its structure beneath the tissue.

I've done this a few times and we try to preserve the capsule as much as possible. However, at some point in time, you usually get into it. Don't be alarmed when you first get into it. There's usually a small amount of serous fluid.
The surgeon begins the retropubic dissection, carefully maneuvering around the obturator and the vas deferens.

The surgeon establishes a critical view of safety for the myopectenial orifice, revealing anatomical landmarks necessary for safe dissection. The focus then shifts to retropubic dissection, continuing the surgical procedure.

The reservoir is visualized outside of its initial capsule, having exited over the pubis. The dissection extends well beyond the midline, with further development of the contralateral dissection. The anatomy of the midline is reviewed.
The surgeon visualizes the reservoir tubing alongside Cooper's ligament, femoral space, indirect space, and surrounding vessels. The mesh is positioned without removing the reservoir.
The surgeon augments the extra-large heavyweight mesh by creating a two-centimeter slit to ensure coverage over the contralateral side.
The surgeon places a mid-weight mesh to address the indirect problem, ensuring appropriate coverage over the neurovascular plane of the obturator (NPO).
The surgeon secures the mesh to the scar tissue, avoiding attachment to Cooper's ligament, ensuring stable placement within the surgical field.
Flap was closed in standard fashion with no major intraoperative issues or problems noted. The total case time for this case took about an hour and thirty minutes.

The patient did very well. We observed them overnight just to make sure things were alright. The patient was just discharged, required no narcotics, resumed anticoagulation within ten days of the procedure, and in surveillance is doing well with no signs of recurrence.

So in conclusion, Patients who have a penile prosthesis and an inguinal hernia can have a minimally invasive approach to repair. It's important to review the implant and plan for your dissection appropriately. As always, whenever performing a MIS, inguinal, important to not compromise the critical view of the myopectinal orifice. Thank you very much.
